narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a white male applicant with a solid middle-income family background and plans to attend law school full-time, you've put real effort into your application, including your undergraduate GPA of 2.90 and LSAT score of 29. Unfortunately, the admissions model, which predicts the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated an 88.3% chance that you would fail it—far too high a risk for the university to approve your admission. This led directly to the rejection of your application, as the school prioritizes students predicted to succeed on the bar. The model's decision hinged most heavily on your academic decile rankings from school: your Year 3 decile of 1 was the strongest factor pushing toward a failure prediction, far below the typical student's mid-range standing around the 5th or 6th decile. Your Year 1 decile of 1 ranked as the second-biggest influence in the same concerning direction, again well under the average. While your undergraduate GPA, slightly below the dataset average of 3.21, offered some counterbalance by nudging the prediction toward passing, it wasn't enough to offset those early academic signals. Your LSAT score, though on the lower side compared to the typical 36.7, had only a minor push toward failure, and your middle family income and full-time status played negligible roles. In essence, compared to typical students who land in higher deciles and show stronger early performance, your profile stood out as riskier for bar success, even with your personal strengths. This isn't a judgment on your potential as a white male from a middle-class background—many in similar situations improve with targeted effort. To move forward, focus on retaking the LSAT to boost your score significantly above average, strengthening your undergraduate record through additional coursework if possible, and seeking academic advising to climb those decile rankings in future endeavors. Reapply after addressing these core areas, and consider programs with holistic reviews that might value your full-time commitment more. You've got resilience ahead—use this feedback as your roadmap to bar success. Keep pushing forward.
